content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 12, 2008
Lori J. Barker
Office of the City Attorney
411 Main Street, 3rd Floor
Chico, CA 95927

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-018

Dear Ms. Barker:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice.¹ (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. Is Councilmember Wahl presumed to have a disqualifying conflict of interest as to decisions on parking in the downtown area of the City of Chico, some of which will be within 500 feet of property owned by his wife?

2. If Councilmember Wahl is presumed to have a conflict of interest, can that presumption be overcome?

3. If the presumption is overcome for some decisions, can those decisions be segregated from the remainder such that Councilmember Wahl may participate in decision-making?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-08-018
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Based on the information provided, Councilmember Wahl does have a disqualifying
conflict of interest.

2. Although there is a presumed disqualification, the presumption may be overcome in
certain instances. There is not enough information provided, however, to determine if
those circumstances are present in this situation.

3. If the presumption is overcome for any of the decisions to be made, those decisions
may be severed from the others so that Councilmember Wahl may take part in the
discussions and decision-making.

FACTS

Councilmember Wahl is an elected member of the Chico City Council. His wife
has an ownership interest in a parcel of real property located in downtown Chico. Her
interest is worth more than $2000. She currently leases the property for use by the
United State Postal Service as a private parking lot.

The Chico City Council will be considering a number of issues related to parking
and parking meters in downtown Chico. The property interest owned by Councilmember
Wahl's wife is located within 500 feet of some of the areas involved in the decisions.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step One: Is Councilmember Wahl a public official?

Under the Act, a public official is "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local government agency." (Section 82048.) As a member of the Chico City
Council, Councilmember Wahl is a public official.

File No. A-08-018
Page No. 3


Step Two: Is Councilmember Wahl making, participating in, or influencing a
governmental decision?

Because he will be called upon to consider decisions relating to the various issues
relating to parking in the city's downtown area, Councilmember Wahl will be making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official position to influence a
governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulations 18702-18702.4.)

Step Three: Does Councilmember Wahl have a potentially disqualifying economic
interest?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain specific economic interests, described in Section 87103 and Regulations
18703-18703.5. A public official has an economic interest:

• In a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a).)

• In a business entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d);
Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• In real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• In any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation
18703.3.)

• In any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts total $390 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

• In his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those
of his or her immediate family. This is commonly referred to as the "personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Property Interest

Under the Act, a real property interest will be considered a financial interest if the
public official has a direct or indirect investment in the property worth two thousand
dollars or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.) This applies to property
interests owned by the public official's spouse. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

File No. A-08-018
Page No. 4

Here, Councilmember Wahl's wife has an ownership interest in a parcel of real
property. The ownership interest is more than $2000. This is a potentially disqualifying
economic interest under the Act.

Business investment

A public official also has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a).) Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise
operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm,
business trust, join venture, syndicate, corporation, or association." Since the
councilmember has an indirect interest in the real property in question and that property
interest is used to produce income leasing it to the Post Office, the councilmember has an
investment interest in the property leasing business of his wife.

Source of Income

Under the Act, a source of income includes a source of rental payments. (Section
82030(a)). Moreover, a public official's "income" for conflict-of-interest purposes
includes his or her community property interest in the income of his or her spouse.
(Section 82030(a).) The property is currently being leased to the United States Postal
Service. Accordingly, if Councilmember Wahl's spouse has received $1,000 or more
from the tenant within 12 months prior a governmental decision, the tenant will be
considered a source of income to the councilmember.

Step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly affected by the decisions?

Real Property

Regulation 18704.2(a)(1) provides that real property in which a public official has
an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if the real property,
or any part of the real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision.
Councilmember Wahl's spouse's property is within 500 feet of some of the areas to be
affected by the decisions considered by the council. Because of this, the economic
interest is directly affected by the decisions.

Source of Income and Business Investment

The standard for determining direct or indirect involvement with respect to
sources of income and business investments is the same:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an

File No. A-08-018
Page No. 5


official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person." (Regulation 18704.1)

Neither the source of income in this case, the USPS, nor the property leasing
business is directly involved in the decision. Neither entity initiated the proceedings nor
are they the subject of the proceedings as contemplated by the Act. When an official's
economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is deemed to be
indirectly involved for purposes of a conflicts-of-interest analysis. (Regulation 18704.1)

Step Five: Will there be a material financial effect on the economic interest involved?

Real Property Interest

Because Councilmember Wahl's spouse's property is directly affected by the
proposed decisions, it is presumed that there will be a material financial effect on the
interest. That presumption may be rebutted by a showing that there is no effect on
the real property value. (Regulation 18705.2)

Source of Income and Business entity

Since both the USPS is indirectly involved in the government decision,
materiality is determined based on the amount of income and the size of the entity.
(Regulation 18705.3) For the USPS, a government entity, Regulation 18705.3(b)(2)
provides that a decision is material if:

"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the
entity's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $
1,000,000 or more; or

"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $ 250,000 or more; or

File No. A-08-018
Page No. 6

"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of
$1,000,000 or more."

With respect to the councilmember's property leasing business, a business entity
that is indirectly involved in the decision, the standard depends on the size of the
business. Regulation 18705.1 provides different materiality standards based on the
financial size of the business. For relatively small businesses (those with net income of
less than $ 500,000 for the most recent fiscal year), the materiality standards set forth in
Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) apply:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."

Of course, if the business actually falls into one of the other categories in
Regulation 18705.1, you would apply the applicable standards.

Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the economic interest will be materially
affected by the decisions?

Conflicts of interest do not arise if a material financial effect is not a reasonably
foreseeable consequence of a particular decision in which the public official participates.
An effect upon an economic interest is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198).

In determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will materially
affect an economic interest, the Commission has developed a number of factors that
should be weighed. (Regulation 18706(b)).

1. The extent to which the official or the official's source of income has
engaged, is engaged, or plans on engaging in business activity in the jurisdiction

File No. A-08-018
Page No. 7


2.     The market share held by the official or the official's source of income in
the jurisdiction
3.     The extent to which the official or the official's source of income has
competition for business in the jurisdiction
4.     The scope of the governmental decision in question
5.     The extent to which the occurrence of the material financial effect is
contingent upon intervening events, not including future governmental decisions
by the official's agency, or any other agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the official's agency

There are seven decisions being discussed at this time. Any decision that can be
shown to not have a material financial effect can be segregated for council action, such
that Councilmember Wahl can participate in the decision-making (see discussion below).
Note, however, that since the property is within 500 feet of the site of the decisions, the
one-penny rule applies, and if even a one penny effect is reasonably foreseeable,
disqualification is required.

The concept of material foreseeability is heavily driven by the particular facts of
each situation as they exist at the time the decision is made. Although we will provide a
preliminary determination on reasonable foreseeability, the determination of whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for your
economic interests is necessarily a factual question that is ultimately one for you to make
based on your understanding of the facts. Additionally, the determination must be made
for each decision under the facts present at the time of the decision. We can offer the
following general analysis. The potential government decisions are discussed in order of
the likelihood of there being a material effect.

•     Changes in the configuration of on-street parking (from parallel to diagonal
parking)

A change in the parking configuration could lead to an increase in parking, which
in turn could raise the value of the property interest, as it may become more accessible to
other potential lessees. There appears to be a substantial likelihood that this decision will
lead to a financial effect in the property interest.

•     Changes in parking meter rates and/or alternative rate schedules
•     Increases in fines for meter violations and fees for late payment of parking tickets.
•     Expanded days/hours for meter operation

There is a possibility that changes in the rates, either by increase or decrease, or
by setting up a graduated rate scale, may have an effect on the value of the property,
especially for those located on the same street as the property, or within 500 feet of it.
Furthermore, the expansion of the days and hours of operation may have an effect on the
value of the property. This decision seems to be aimed at bringing more cars into the

File No. A-08-018
Page No. 8

downtown area, especially if the time limits either do not change or decrease. An
increase in people in the general area could have a financial effect on the property

• Installation of an alternative type of parking meter (from coin operated to kiosks)
• Changes in enforcement methods to be more efficient
• Hiring a private company to provide meter enforcement (as opposed to using the
City's Police Department as is currently done)

While there is a possibility that these decisions may have a financial effect on the
property interest, the requirement is that there is a substantial likelihood that an effect
will occur. It is unlikely that these decisions will lead to a financial effect on the property
interest.

To determine the foreseeability of a material effect on the indirectly involved
source of income and business investment, the same factors and analysis would be
applied. However, instead of looking for a one-penny effect, you would need to again
determine what standard of materiality applies under regulation 18705.3(b)(2). It is
unlikely that there will be the necessary financial effect under that regulation.
However, if any of the decisions will have such an effect, Councilmember Wahl must
disqualify himself from those specific decisions and must leave the room.²

Steps Seven and Eight: Are there any exceptions to the conflicts of interest rules?

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. The "legally required
participation" rule applies when the official's participation in a governmental decision is
legally required. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.) You have not presented any facts
indicating that these exceptions are applicable to your situation; therefore we do not
address them here.

Segregation of Decisions

You ask about the possibility of segregating some of the decisions for council
action, then allowing Councilmember Wahl to participate in those he does not have a
conflict of interest in. We have previously advised that large and complex decisions may,

² When a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in
a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.

File No. A-08-018
Page No. 9

under certain circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so that an official who has
a disqualifying economic interest in one component of the decision may still participate
in other components in which the official has no disqualifying economic interest.
(Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542; Huffacker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.) In
such cases, the following procedure should be followed:

1. The decisions for which the official has a disqualifying conflict of interest must be
segregated from the other decisions in which he does not have a disqualifying
conflict of interest;

2. The decisions for which the official has a disqualifying conflict of interest must
be considered first, and a final decision reached without his involvement;

3. Once a decision has been reached on the matters for which he or she has a
disqualifying conflict of interest, the official may be involved in the deliberations
regarding remaining portions of the overall matter for which he or she does not
have a disqualifying conflict of interest, as long as those deliberations do not
result in a reopening, or in any way affect, the decisions from which he or she is
disqualified. (Huffaker Advice Letter, supra.)

In this instance, the decisions have already been divided into several smaller
issues. If any of the above decisions are found to not have a disqualifying conflict of
interest, then Councilmember Wahl may be permitted to take part in the decision-making
process on those specific issues. However, he may only do so after those decisions for
which he has a disqualifying conflict are considered.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Pamela Azar
Legal Intern, Legal Division

PA:jgl

interest is used to produce income leasing it to the Post Office, the councilmember has an
investment interest in the property leasing business of his wife.

Under the Act, a source of income includes a source of rental payments. (Section
82030(a)). Moreover, a public official's "income" for conflict-of-interest purposes
includes his or her community property interest in the income of his or heispouse.
(Section 82030(a).) The property is currently being leased to rhe United Staies postal

sollrces of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an

filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar reqLlest or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the

person." (Regulation 1 8704. 1)
Neither the source of income in this case, the USPS, nor the property leasing

Step Five: Will there be a material financial effect on the economic interest involved?
Real Property Interest
Because Councilmember Wahl's spouse's property is directly affected by the

interest. That:presumption may be rebutted through a showing that there is no effect on
the real property value. (Regulation 18705.2)

Since both the USPS is indirectly involved in the government decision,

(Regulation 18705.3) For the USPS, a government entity, Regulation 18705.3(bX2)
provides that a decision is material if:

entity's gross annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $
1,000,000 or more; or
"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $ 250,000 or more; or
"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of
$1,000,000 or more."
With respect to the councilmember's property leasing business, a business entity
that is indirectly involved in the decision, the standard depends on the size of the
business. Regulation 18705.1 provides different materialiiy standards based on the
financial size of the business. For relatively small businesies (those with net income of
less than $ 500,000 for the most recent fiscal year), the materiality standards set forth in
Regulation 18705. 1(c)(4) apply:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in
the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of
$5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Of course, if the business actually falls into one of the other categories in
Regulation 18705.1, you would apply the applicable standards.

following general analysis. The potential govemment decisions are discussed in order of
the likelihood of there being a material effect.
changes in the configuration of on-street parking (from parallel to diagonal
parking)
a
A change in the parking configuration could lead to an increase in parking, which
in turn could raise the value of the property interest, as it may become .o." ,."".sible to
other potential lessees. There appears to be a substantial likelihood that this decision will
lead to a financial effect in the property interest.
o changes in parking meter rates and./or alternative rate schedules
o Increases in fines for meter violations and fees for late payment of parking tickets.
o Expanded days/trours for meter operation
There is a possibility that changes in the rates, either by increase or decrease, or
by setting up a graduated rate scale, may have an effect on thsvalue of the property,
especially for those located on the same street as the property, or within SOO felt of it.
Furthermore, the expansion of the days and hours of operation may have an effect on the
value of the property. This decision seems to be aimed at bringing more cars into the
downtown area, especially if the time limits either do not change or decrease. An
increase in people in the general area could have a financial effict on the property
o Installation of an alternative type of parking meter (from coin operated to kiosks)
o Changes in enforcement methods to be more efficient
o Hiring a private company to provide meter enforcement (as opposed to using the
City's Police Department as is currently done)
While there is a possibility that these decisions may have a financial effect on the
property interest, the requirement is that there is a substantial likelihood thatan effect
will occur. It is unlikely that these decisions will lead to a financial effect on the properry
interest.
To determine the foreseeability of a material effect on the indirectly involved
source of income and business investment, the same factors and analysis would be
applied. However, instead of looking for a one-penny effect, you would need to again
determine what standard of materiality applies under regulation 18705.3(b)(2). It is
unlikely that that there will be the necessary financial effect under that reguiaiion.
However, if any of the decisions will have such an effect, Councilmembei Wahl must
disqualify himself from those specific decisions and must leave the room.2
Steps Seven and Eight: Are there any exceptions to the conflicts of interest rules?
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interesti is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. The "legally required
participation" rule applies when the official's participation in a govemmental decision is
legally required. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.) You have not presented any facts
indicating that these exceptions are applicable to your situation; theiefore we do not
address them here.
S e g re gation of Dec is ions
You ask about the possibility of segregating some of the decisions for council
action, then allowing Councilmember Wahl to participate in those he does not have a
conflict of interest in. We have previously advised that large and complex decisions may,
. ' When a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in
a decision noticed at a public meeting. then he or she must: ( l) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economicinterest,asdiscussedinregulation 18702.5(b),ontherecordof themeeting;(2)recusehimself or
herself: and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and 1g702.5(d) apply.
under certain circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so that an official who has
a disqualifying economic interest in one component of the decision may still participate
in other components in which the official has no disqualifying economic interest.
(Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542; Huffacker Advice Letter, No. A-g6-343.) In
such cases, the following procedure should be followed:
1. The decisions for which the official has a disqualifying conflict of interest must
be segregated from the other decisions in which he does not have a disqualifying
conflict of interest;
2. The decisions for which the official has a disqualifying conflict of interest must
be considered first, and a final decision reached without his involvement;
3. Once a decision has been reached on the matters for which he or she has a
disqualifying conflict of interest, the official may be involved in the deliberations
regarding remaining portions of the overall matter for which he or she does not
have a disqualifying conflict of interest, as long as those deliberations do not
result in a reopening, or in any way affect, the decisions from which he or she is
disqualified. (Huffaker Advice Letter, supra.)
In this instance, the decisions have already been divided into several smaller
issues. If any of the above decisions are found to not have a disqualifying conflict of
interest, then Councilmember Wahl may be permitted to take part in the decision-making
process on those specific issues. However, he may only do so after those decisions for
which he has a disqualifying conflict are considered.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 12, 2008 Lori J. Barker Office of the City Attorney 411 Main Street, 3rd Floor Chico, CA 95927 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-08-018 Dear Ms. Barker: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice.¹ (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTIONS 1. Is Councilmember Wahl presumed to have a disqualifying conflict of interest as to decisions on parking in the downtown area of the City of Chico, some of which will be within 500 feet of property owned by his wife? 2. If Councilmember Wahl is presumed to have a conflict of interest, can that presumption be overcome? 3. If the presumption is overcome for some decisions, can those decisions be segregated from the remainder such that Councilmember Wahl may participate in decision-making? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-08-018 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. Based on the information provided, Councilmember Wahl does have a disqualifying conflict of interest. 2. Although there is a presumed disqualification, the presumption may be overcome in certain instances. There is not enough information provided, however, to determine if those circumstances are present in this situation. 3. If the presumption is overcome for any of the decisions to be made, those decisions may be severed from the others so that Councilmember Wahl may take part in the discussions and decision-making. FACTS Councilmember Wahl is an elected member of the Chico City Council. His wife has an ownership interest in a parcel of real property located in downtown Chico. Her interest is worth more than $2000. She currently leases the property for use by the United State Postal Service as a private parking lot. The Chico City Council will be considering a number of issues related to parking and parking meters in downtown Chico. The property interest owned by Councilmember Wahl's wife is located within 500 feet of some of the areas involved in the decisions. ANALYSIS Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
